course.title: AI Foundations: From Algorithms to AI Factories
course.subtitle: A full-stack course on the software and hardware that produced modern AI
course.description: A comprehensive course covering the research, systems, hardware, and future directions behind modern artificial intelligence.
targetAudience: Learners with basic computer science knowledge
Engineers, founders, researchers, and curious self-learners

modules.title: Foundations and Historical Context
modules.description: The conceptual and mathematical foundations of AI, from early ideas to core machine learning.

modules.title: Classical AI, Uncertainty, and Sequential Decision-Making
modules.description: Search, planning, probabilistic reasoning, causality, and reinforcement learning.

modules.title: Deep Learning Foundations
modules.description: Neural networks, optimization, vision, sequence models, and generative modeling.

modules.title: Foundation Models and Modern AI Systems
modules.description: Pretraining, transformers, post-training, retrieval, tools, agents, and multimodality.

modules.title: The Hardware and Systems Stack
modules.description: Numerical computing, chips, accelerators, distributed systems, and AI datacenters.

modules.title: Safety, Interpretability, and the Future
modules.description: Robustness, governance, and frontier research directions.

lessons.title: What AI is: history, paradigms, and why the field looks the way it does now
lessons.shortDescription: Map the field from symbolic AI to foundation models and understand why different paradigms rose and fell.
learningObjectives: Explain the major paradigms of AI.
Define representation, learning, reasoning, and generalization.
Place modern foundation models in historical context.

papers.title: Computing Machinery and Intelligence
papers.whyItMatters: Frames machine intelligence as a testable question.

papers.title: A Proposal for the Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence
papers.whyItMatters: Defines the original AI research agenda.

papers.title: Some Studies in Machine Learning Using the Game of Checkers
papers.whyItMatters: Early example of systems that improve from experience.

papers.title: Machine learning: Trends, perspectives, and prospects
papers.whyItMatters: Modern retrospective on how machine learning reshaped AI.

lessonPlan.summary: Map the field from symbolic AI to foundation models and understand why different paradigms rose and fell.

sections.heading: Why this lesson matters
sections.content: Map the field from symbolic AI to foundation models and understand why different paradigms rose and fell.

sections.heading: Core ideas
sections.content: Expand this section into a full lesson narrative during content authoring.

sections.heading: Practical implications
sections.content: Connect the theory in this lesson to modern AI systems and research practice.

takeaways: Explain the major paradigms of AI.
Define representation, learning, reasoning, and generalization.
Place modern foundation models in historical context.

quiz.prompt: What is the central learning goal of 'What AI is: history, paradigms, and why the field looks the way it does now'?
choices: Understanding the core concepts introduced in the lesson
Memorizing implementation syntax only
Ignoring historical context and evaluation
Replacing all prior AI methods with one technique
quiz.explanation: Each lesson is built to teach core concepts, context, and practical implications.

lessons.title: Mathematical foundations for AI
lessons.shortDescription: Cover the minimum optimization, probability, and information theory needed to read AI papers with confidence.
learningObjectives: Use entropy, cross-entropy, and KL divergence correctly.
Explain empirical risk minimization and stochastic optimization.
Relate numerical precision to training stability.

papers.title: A Mathematical Theory of Communication
papers.whyItMatters: Introduces entropy and information as measurable quantities.

papers.title: On Information and Sufficiency
papers.whyItMatters: Defines KL divergence as a way to compare distributions.

papers.title: A Stochastic Approximation Method
Optimization Methods for Large-Scale Machine Learning
papers.whyItMatters: Practical framework for optimization at modern ML scale.

lessonPlan.summary: Cover the minimum optimization, probability, and information theory needed to read AI papers with confidence.

sections.heading: Why this lesson matters

lessons.title: Statistical learning theory and evaluation
lessons.shortDescription: Learn how models generalize, how benchmarks fail, and how evaluation should be designed.
learningObjectives: Explain generalization and PAC-style learning intuitions.
Use the bias-variance framework appropriately.
Distinguish accuracy from calibration and robustness.

papers.title: A Theory of the Learnable
papers.whyItMatters: Formalizes what it means for a problem to be learnable.

papers.title: Neural Networks and the Bias/Variance Dilemma
papers.whyItMatters: Classic explanation of underfitting and overfitting.

papers.title: Do CIFAR-10 Classifiers Generalize to CIFAR-10?
papers.whyItMatters: Shows why benchmark success can be fragile.

papers.title: On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks
papers.whyItMatters: Demonstrates why confidence and correctness often diverge.

lessons.title: Core machine learning algorithms
lessons.shortDescription: Understand the pre-deep-learning toolbox and why it still matters.
learningObjectives: Compare linear models, SVMs, trees, forests, and boosting.
Know when tabular methods beat deep nets.
Differentiate supervised from unsupervised learning.

papers.title: Support-Vector Networks
Random Forests
papers.whyItMatters: Shows how bagging and tree randomness improve generalization.

papers.title: Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine
Some Methods for Classification and Analysis of Multivariate Observations

papers.whyItMatters: Connects causality to modern representation learning.
